Facts.text: 5th recorded perihelion passage of Halley's Comet
"Pentateuch" the Jewish Bible is 1st printed as a book in Bologna, Italy
Vicente Yáñez Pinzón becomes the first European to set foot in Brazil
Lisbon hit by Earthquake; about 30,000 die
The Council of Trent issued its conclusions in the Tridentinum, establishing a distinction between Roman Catholicism and Protestantism
Job is elected as Patriarch of Moscow and All Russia
Portuguese troops conquer last Dutch base on Recife
France declares war on England & Munster
Venice, Poland & Austria sign Treaty of Karlowitz with Ottoman Empire, ending the Great Turkish War (1683-97) - 1st time Ottomans concede significant territory
The magnitude 8.7-9.2 Cascadia earthquake took place off the west coast of the North America, as evidenced by Japanese records
Stanislaw I of Poland abdicates his throne
Britain, Netherlands, Austria & Sardinia sign anti-French treaty
John Odell signs contract for £336 to build St Peter's church in the Bronx
Russia, Prussia and Austria sign treaty
Congress passes an act calling for a US Capitol library
Rum Rebellion, the only successful (albeit short-lived) armed takeover of the government in Australia
Michigan admitted as 26th US state
Myall Creek Massacre: about 50 Wirrayaraay indigenous people killed by New South Wales Mounted Police (seven men later the first ever to be hanged for killing Australian Aborigines)
Tennessee enacts the first prohibition law in the United States
Hong Kong proclaimed a sovereign territory of Britain
1st German language daily newspaper in US published, NYC
Édouard-Léon Scott de Martinville delivers his design for a phonautograph, which created visual images of sound, to the French Academy
Louisiana secedes from the Union (US Civil War)
American Civil War: Massachusetts Governor receives permission from Secretary of War to raise a militia organization for men of African descent (54th Massachusetts Regiment)
US Congress authorises Virginia to rejoin the union
The Rugby Football Union is formed at a meeting of 21 clubs at the Pall Mall Restaurant, London, England
Electric dental drill is patented by George F Green
Union of Baptists Communities forms in Foxholl
France government of Gambetta falls
1st Dutch Wagner version of Elizabeth aria
Muhammad Ahmed ("Mahdi") rebels conquer Khartoum
Battle of Dogali: Abyssinian Emperor John IV defeats Italians
Oscar Wilde's tragic play "Duchess of Padua" premieres at the Broadway Theater in NYC
Battle at Bida Gold Coast: British troops beat Nupe's army
Han Yong-woon [Bongwan, Manhae] (1879-1944) ordained a monk in Korea
1st US federal corrupt election practices law passed
In Austria, universal and direct suffrage (limited to males over 24) is introduced, a reform long demanded by the socialists and others
John Millington Synge's "Playboy of Western World" opens in Dublin; play claimed as immoral and causes riots
The Short Magazine Lee-Enfield Mk III rifle officially introduced into British Military Service, 2nd oldest military rifle still in official use
Heavy rains cause floods in Paris
Glenn Curtiss pilots 1st successful hydroplane, in San Diego, California
600 Dutch textile workers go on strike
Vatican puts Belgian Nobel winner Maeterlinck's works in their index
Rocky Mountain National Park, Colorado, is established
Amedeo Modigliani's fiancé Jeanne Hébuterne jumps out of a window a day after the artist's funeral killing herself and her unborn child

American Henry Leland's military engine manufacturing company, the Lincoln Motor Company reorganizes as an automobile manufacturer; he sells the struggling company to the Ford Motor Company in 1922yer
Toronto NHL center Corb Denneny scores 6 goals to lead St. Patricks to a 10-3 win over Hamilton Tigers in Toronto
Maxwell Anderson's play "Saturday's Children" premieres in New York City
Indian National Congress proclaims goal for India's independence
Cleveland's Terminal Tower opens (52 stories)
Hungary-Austria sign peace treaty
Lynn Riggs' "Green Grow the Lilacs" premieres in NYC
British submarine M-2 sinks in English Channel (60 dead)
KUT-AM in Austin Texas changes call letters to KNOW
Nazi Germany & Poland sign 10-year non-aggression treaty
The Apollo Theater reopens in Harlem, New York City.
Federal Hall National Monument established
Filming begins on film adaptation of "Gone With The Wind"
Nazis forbid Polish Jews to travel on trains
1st US force in Europe during WWII go ashore in Northern Ireland
KLM Royal Dutch Airlines Douglas DC-3 crashes shortly after takeoff from Kastrup Airport near Copenhagen, Denmark, 16 passengers and 6 crew members die
200" (5.08-m) Hale telescope 1st used at Palomar observatory in Palomar Mountain, California [see also June 3, 1948]
Australia's Nationality and Citizenship Act 1948 comes into force granting Australian citizenship and nationality to all people born or naturalised in Australia including First Nations people
WHIO TV channel 7 in Dayton, OH (CBS) begins broadcasting
At least 20 people have been killed and 100s injured during riots in Cairo against the British
Groundbreaking begins on Disneyland
Porkkala military base returned to Finland by USSR
VII Winter Olympic Games open in Cortina d'Ampezzo, Italy
Dutch Pacifist Socialist Party forms
Francis Poulenc's opera "Dialogue des Carmelites" premieres at La Scala Teatro in Milan, Italy
India annexes Kashmir
Joseph F Cairnes succeeds Lou Perini as President of Milwaukee Braves
Jack Smith takes over from Art Baker as TV host of "You Asked for It"
Japanese ferry Nankai Maru capsized off southern Awaji Island, Japan, 167 killed.
Italy government of Fanfani resigns
KOKH TV channel 25 in Oklahoma City, OK (IND/PBS) begins broadcasting
High-school basketball sensation Danny Heater scores 135 points
Oakland Raiders enter the American Football League
Canadian Marine Service renamed Coast Guard
David Diamond's 7th Symphony premieres with the Philadelphia Orchestra conducted by Eugene Ormandy
US launches Ranger 3, misses Moon by 22,000-mi (37,000-km)
Major League Rules Committee votes to expand strike zone
South Vietnam military coup under general Nguyen Khanh
The Beaumont Children go missing from Glenelg Beach near Adelaide, South Australia.
Chicago Blizzard strikes with a record 23 inches of snow fall causing 800 buses and 50,000 automobiles to be abandoned
USSR performs nuclear test at Sary Shagan USSR
Israeli submarine Dakar sinks in Mediterranean Sea, 69 die
Minister of Health and Social Services William Morgan resigns from the Northern Ireland government
Police break up a march by hundreds of demonstrators in central Prague
"Bridge over Troubled Water" 5th and final studio album by Simon & Garfunkel is released (Grammy Award Album of the Year, Best Engineered Non-Classical Album 1971)
Pendleton, Ford & Cryer's "Last Sweet Days of Isaac," premieres in NYC
Dutch 2nd Chamber accepts law against limitation of war crimes
Aboriginal Tent Embassy protest for land rights begins on the lawn of Parliament House, Canberra, Australia. Longest continuous protest for indigenous rights in the world.
Serbian air stewardess Vesna Vulovic survives 10,160m fall without parachute - world's highest fall without a parachute
Belgium government of Leburton forms
Belgium Catholic elite starts amnesty campaign for war criminals
Israel opens "Good Fence" to Lebanon
Soviet figure skaters Sergei Shakrai & Marine Tcherkasova are 1st to perform a quadruple twist lift in Helsinki
International Ultraviolet Explorer placed in Earth orbit
Strikers riot in Tunisia, killing about 40
"The Dukes of Hazzard" premieres on US TV network CBS
Music Center Vredenburg opens in Utrecht, Netherlands
Islanders & Whalers play a NHL penalty-free game
Israel & Egypt establish diplomatic relations
Mary Decker became 1st woman to run a mile in under 4½ minutes
Sandeep Patil scores memorable 174 v Australia at Adelaide Oval
For the first time since the 1930s, the unemployed population in UK rises above three million
In a 9-2 NHL win over Pittsburgh Penguins, the NY Islanders score 4 goals within 1:38, 5 within 2:37 in a 6 goal 1st period
Mauno Koivisto elected president of Finland
Dutch British infrared satellite IRAS launched from California
Nordiques' Michel Goulet scored on 9th penalty shot against Islanders
US Navy exhibits experimental Piasecki Helistat - a combination of 4 helicopters and a blimp - able to lift 26 tons at  Naval Air Station in Lakehurst, New Jersey
Hein Vergeer becomes European skating champion
Super Bowl XX, Louisiana Superdome, New Orleans, Louisiana: Chicago Bears beat New England Patriots, 46-10; MVP: Richard Dent, Chicago, DE
Yoweri Museveni's rebel army conquers Kampala, Uganda
Australian 200th anniversary parade of tall ships in Sydney Harbour
AT&T reports 1st loss in 103 years; $1.67 B in 1988
Madison Square Garden announces 2-year $100m renovation plan
Test debut of Mark Taylor, Australia v West Indies, Sydney
US computer security expert warns of catastrophic virus
Annular eclipse visible over Antarctica & South Atlantic
Boston Red Sox hires Elaine Weddington as asst GM (highest-ranking African American female in a major-league front office)
Alfaro Vive guerrilla group of Ecuador gives arms to Catholic church
Jan Stenerud becomes 1st pure placekicker to make NFL Hall of Fame
NY Lotto pays $90 million to nine winners (#s are 5-15-30-35-46-50)
Super Bowl XXVI, Metrodome, Minneapolis, MI: Washington Redskins beat Buffalo Bills, 37-24; MVP: Mark Rypien, Washington, QB
Everton scores a 3-1 away win over Wimbledon at Selhurst Park in London; lowest ever attendance at an EPL fixture, 3,039
West Indies defeat Australia by one run in 4th Test at Adelaide
With a record 6,254,427 ballots cast by fans in NBA All-Star Voting, Detroit's Grant Hill becomes the first rookie to lead all NBA players in All-Star votes received, earning #1 on 1,289,585 ballots
Brunswick World Bowling Tournament of Champions won by John Gant
Super Bowl XXXI, Louisiana Superdome, New Orleans, LA: Green Bay Packers beat New England Patriots, 35-21; MVP: Desmond Howard, Green Bay KR
An earthquake hits Gujarat, India, causing more than 20,000 deaths.
Super Bowl XXXVII, Qualc:omm Stadium, San Diego, CA: Tampa Bay Buccaneers beat Oakland Raiders, 48-21; MVP: Dexter Jackson, Tampa Bay, S
A whale explodes in the town of Tainan, Taiwan. A build-up of gas in the decomposing sperm whale is suspected of causing the explosion.
Mydoom, the most destructive computer worm (so far), first sighted on computers in North America. Goes on to cause $38 billion in damages.
Glendale train crash: Two trains derail killing 11 and injuring 200 in Glendale, California, near Los Angeles.
Western Union discontinues its telegram service.
"I Run to You" single by Lady Antebellum is released (Grammy Award Best Country Performance by a Duo or Group with Vocal 2009, Billboard Song of the Year 2009)
After 6 consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth, the UK economy officially finally comes out of recession with a GDP growth of 0.1%
10 people are killed and 20 are injured by a suicide bombing in Kunduz, Afghanistan
30 people are killed during a violent protest in Port Said, Egypt
Miloš Zeman wins the Czech Republic’s presidential election
The United States Sentencing Commission is hacked by Anonymous in response to the suicide of Aaron Swartz
21 people are killed after a tourist boat capsizes off the Andaman and Nicobar Islands
Libby Lane is ordained as the first female bishop of the Church of England
Cold snap across East Asia leads to more than 50 deaths and 60,000 travellers stranded in South Korea
Leader of Oregon militia occupying Malheur wildlife refuge arrested and another killed in shoot-out with Federal agents
Residents left without heat in -27C in Bishkek, Kyrgyzstan after boiler breaks in main power station
Chew Valley Hoard of 2,528 silver coins from the Norman conquest is found near Bath - the highest-value treasure find ever in England at £4.3m ($5.6 million)
Five people shot and killed in domestic deputes in Louisiana, a 21- year old gunman arrested a day later
US Supreme Court Justice Stephen Breyer announces his retirement
Five ex-Memphis police officers charged with murdering Tyre Nichols who they beat at a traffic stop on Jan 7
English kingEdward IIIproclaimed king of France
Jean Racine's play "Esther" premieres, performed by pupils of the Maison royale de Saint-Louis, in Saint-Cyr
Isaac Newtonreceives Jean Bernoulli's 6 month time-limit problem, solves problem before going to bed that same night
CaptainJames Cookstops in Van Diemen's Land (now Tasmania) with Resolution, for supplies, on his 3rd trip to Pacific Ocean
Benjamin Franklinexpresses unhappiness over eagle as America's symbol
CaptainArthur Phillipand British colonists hoist the Union Flag at Sydney Cove, New South Wales, now celebrated as Australia Day. Referred to as Invasion Day by some First Nations people.
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart's opera "Cosi Fan Tutte" premieres at the Burgtheater in Vienna, Austria
Lincoln issues General War Order #1, calling for a Union offensive, GeneralGeorge McClellanignores order
Ground is broken and construction begins on the Eiffel Tower in Paris, France
Alexander Gretchaninov's 1st Symphony premieres in Saint Petersburg, Russia, with Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov conducting
Henrik Ibsen's play "Naar vi Dode Vaaguer" (When We Dead Awaken) premieres in Stuttgart
Richard Strauss's opera "Der Rosenkavalier" (Knight of the Rose) premieres at the Königliches Opernhaus in Dresden, Germany
Jim Thorperelinquishes his 1912 Olympic medals for playing 2 seasons of semi-professional baseball before competing in the Olympics
US Food AdministratorHerbert Hoovercalls for "wheatless" and "meatless" days to help war effort
Clas Thunbergof Finland claims his first of 5 gold medals when he wins the 5,000m speed skating in 2:20.8 at the Chamonix Winter Olympics
John Logie Bairdgives the first public demonstration of television in his laboratory in London
Rube Goldberg's "The Inventions of Professor Lucifer Gorgonzola Butts, A.K.," cartoon series first published in "Colliers Weekly"
"Cimarron" directed by Wesley Ruggles and starring Richard Dix andIrene Dunnepremieres in New York. 1st western to win Best Outstanding Production/Picture (1931)
Joseph StalinviewsDmitri Shostakovich's opera "Lady Macbeth" but he and his officials leave early and the play is denounced in state press
Francisco Franco's Nationalist troops conquer Barcelona
Italian supreme command demands dismissal of German Field MarshalErwin Rommel
Constitution of independent India comes into effect;Rajendra Prasadelected India's first president
Mel OttandJimmie Foxxare elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame
Hank GreenbergandJoe Croninare elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame
Haim Laskow replacesMoshe Dayanas Israeli Chief of the General Staff - in charge of Israeli Defense Forces
Pete Rozelleelected NFL commissioner on 23rd ballot
"Are You Lonesome Tonight?" byElvis Presleyhits #1
Janet G. Travell is 1st woman named as personal physician to a US President (John F. Kennedy)
Dutch speed skaterArd Schenkskates world record 1500m (2:06.2)
Bülent Ecevitbecomes Prime Minister of Turkey for the 1st time
Edward Albee's play "Seascape" premieres in NYC
David Mamet's play "American Buffalo" premieres in NYC
Frank Herbertcompletes his novel "Destination: Void"
Mário Soaresforms Portuguese government
175,000 pay to hearFrank Sinatrasing in Rio de Janeiro
Nazi Gestapo war criminalKlaus Barbiearrested in Bolivia
Edmonton OilerWayne Gretzkyscores 50th goal in 49th game of season
NJ Governor Christine Whitman, dedicates a rest stop on Interstate I-295 to radio personalityHoward Stern
Mario Lemieuxties NHL record with 4 goals in the 3rd period as Pittsburgh wins, 5-2 at Montreal; 39th career hat trick and 13th 4-goal game, tying NHL record shared by Wayne Gretzky & Mike Bossy
Intellaunches 333 MHz Pentium II chip
American late-night talk show "Jimmy KimmelLive!" debuts on ABC-TV
PresidentHamid Karzaisigns the new constitution of Afghanistan
Condoleezza Riceis sworn in as U.S. Secretary of State, the first African American woman to hold the post
"Blame It (On the Alcohol)" single released byJamie FoxxfeaturingT-Pain(Billboard Song of the Year 2009)
56th Grammy Awards:Macklemore& Ryan Lewis,Lorde, and Daft Punk win
62nd Grammy Awards:Billie Eilishwins 5 Grammys, including album, record and song of the year

LA Lakers basketball legendKobe Bryantand his daughter Gianna Bryant dies in a helicopter crash in foggy conditions in the hills above Calabasas, southern California; considered one of the greatest players in the game's history
Antony Blinkenconfirmed by US Senate as Secretary of State
Spotify removesNeil Young's music from its streaming platform after the singer-songwriter's ultimatum in objection to COVID-19 misinformation inJoe Rogan's podcasts
US Federal jury saysDonald Trumpmust pay $83 million in damages to E. Jean Carroll after he denied raping her
World's largest diamond, the 3,106-carat Cullinan, is found in South Africa
American skater Charles Jewtraw claims the first ever Winter Olympic gold medal; wins 500m speed skating event in 44.0s at the Chamonix Games in France
Bishop Burke of Buffalo Catholic dioceses declaresChubby Checker's "The Twist" to be impure and bans it from all Catholic schools
President Bill Clinton says "I want to say one thing to the American people; I did not have sexual relations with that woman,Miss Lewinsky"
The World Health Organization rejects claims that it overstated the severity of the swine flu pandemic under pressure from vaccine companies
Go-Nara, 105th Emperor of Japan (1526-57) during the Sengoku period, born in Japan (d. 1557)
Florent Chrestien, French writer and satirist, born in Orléans, France (d. 1596)
Johann Jakob Wolleb, Swiss organist, theologian and composer, born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1667)
Henricus Zwaardecroon, Dutch Governor-General of Dutch East Indies (1718-25) who introduced coffee crop to Java, born in Rotterham, Dutch Republic (d. 1728)
William Hayes, British composer and organist, baptized in Gloucester, England (d. 1777)
Claude Adrien Helvétius, French controversial philosopher (On the Mind), born in Paris, France (d. 1771)
George Sackville Germain, 1st Viscount Sackville, British soldier and politician (Secretary of State for the Americas during War of Independence), born in London, England (d. 1785)
Alexander Carlyle, Scottish church leader (Moderator of the General Assembly 1770), born in Cummertrees, Dumfriesshire (d. 1805)
Johann Friedrich Ludwig Sievers, German composer, born in Hanover, Electorate of Hanover, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1806)
Emanuel Aloys Förster, Bohemian composer and music teacher, born in Niederstaina, Kingdom of Prussia (now Ścinawka Dolna, Poland) (d. 1823)

Mary Mapes Dodge, American children's author (St. Nicholas Magazine), editor and writer (Hans Brinker, or The Silver Skates), born in New York City (d. 1905)

Julius H. Hess, American physician who is often considered the father of American neonatology (Michael Reese Hospital, Chicago), born in Ottawa, Illinois (d. 1955)
Kees van Dongen, Dutch-French painter (Fauves style), born in Rotterdam Netherlands (d. 1968)
Lode Baekelmans, Belgian writer (Tille), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1965)

Roy Chapman Andrews, American scientist and explorer (found 1st known dinosaur eggs, director of the American Museum of Natural History), born in Beloit, Wisconsin (d. 1960)
Walter "Doc" Meanwell, English Basketball Hall of Fame coach (University of Wisconsin 1911–1917, 1920–1934), born in Leeds, England (d. 1953)
Arnout Colnot, Dutch painter and graphic artist, born in Amsterdam (d. 1983)

Charles Scribner III, American book publisher, born in New York City (d. 1952)

Ancel Keys, American nutritionist and physiologist who established connection between diet and heart disease and strokes, born in Colorado Springs (d. 2004)[1]

Eddie Ballantine, American orchestra leader (Don McNeill TV Club), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1995)

William Prince, American actor (Spies Like Us, The Stepford Wives, City in Fear), born in Nichols, New York, (d. 1996)

Anne "Biddy" Macfarlane (née Griffiths), British solicitor and 1st female appointed as Master of the Court of Protection, born in England (d. 2019)
Anthony Nathan Solomons, British banking chairman (Singer & Friedlander), born in London

Roger Landry, Canadian businessman and newspaper publisher (La Presse), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2000)

Henry Jordan, American Pro Football HOF defensive tackle (Super Bowl 1967, 68; 7 × All-Pro; 4 × Pro Bowl; Cleveland Browns, GB Packers), born in Emporia, Virginia (d. 1977)
